26: A shadow: 'Let us make humanity in our image, after our likeness.' The imago Dei in Genesis is the first shadow of the ultimate image of God, who is Christ (2 Corinthians 4:4; Colossians 1:15). Humanity was created to bear God's image — to represent God in the world. The Fall distorted the image without destroying it (Genesis 9:6 still assumes the image as the basis for human dignity). Christ is the image that humanity was always meant to embody: perfectly representing the Father, exercising dominion over creation, fulfilling the cultural mandate. The redemption of humanity is therefore the restoration of the image in and through the one who is himself the image — conformity to Christ is the telos of creation.
1: The opening words “In the beginning” are explicitly taken up in John 1:1—“In the beginning was the Word—“ to identify Christ as the eternal divine agent present at creation’s origin. The theme of creation-through-the-Word is developed throughout John’s prologue and in Colossians 1:16 (“all things were created through him and for him”) and Hebrews 1:2 (God “made the universe through” the Son). This verse is not a prophecy about Christ but the narrative ground of which Christ is the revealed protagonist: the God who created in the beginning is the God who redeems through his Son, who was present at that same beginning.
2: The Spirit of God (rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm) hovering over the formless waters brings order and life from chaos. The same Spirit descends on Christ at his baptism (Mark 1:10 — the Spirit like a dove), consecrating him for his mission of bringing new creation order out of sin’s chaos. The creation-Spirit and the Christ-Spirit are one: Paul calls the Spirit who raised Jesus from the dead “the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead” (Romans 8:11). The Spirit’s hover over the primordial deep is the first movement of the redemptive purpose that culminates in Christ.
3: “Let there be light” is taken up by Paul as a direct type of the gospel: “God, who said, ‘Let light shine out of darkness,’ made his light shine in our hearts to give us the light of the knowledge of God’s glory displayed in the face of Christ” (2 Corinthians 4:6). The creative word that produced physical light is the same divine word that produces spiritual illumination in the face of Jesus. Christ himself claims the fulfillment: “I am the light of the world” (John 8:12; Genesis 9:5). The theme of light-from-darkness is the first theme in Scripture and the defining theme of John’s Gospel.
4: God separated the light from the darkness — the first act of divine judgment in Scripture, distinguishing what is good (light) from what is not. Christ comes as the one in whom this separation reaches its moral and eschatological fulfillment: “The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it” (John 1:5). The judgment that separates light from darkness anticipates the final judgment when Christ will separate the sheep from the goats (Matthew 25:32). The theme established here — light and darkness as moral categories — is the trajectory that ends in Christ’s victory over darkness (1 John 2:8).
5: The first day establishes the evening-morning pattern that structures all of creation week. The theme of God bringing light-day out of darkness-evening runs through Scripture toward the ultimate Day that Christ inaugurates: “the day of the Lord” (1 Thessalonians 5:2), the “morning star” who is Christ himself (Revelation 22:16; 2 Peter 1:19). This transitional verse does not directly anticipate Christ but establishes the day-night framework that becomes a pervasive metaphor for salvation history moving from darkness toward the light that Christ brings permanently.
6: God’s word creates structure and order in the heavens. The theme of God’s word ordering reality points toward Christ as the Word through whom “all things were made” (John 1:3) and in whom “all things hold together” (Colossians 1:17). The expanse that separates waters is a work of divine order-making; Christ’s calming of the sea (Mark 4:39) and walking on water (Matthew 14:25) reveals him as the lord of the same waters over which God exercised sovereignty here.
7: The making of the expanse and the ordering of the waters above and below establishes God as the sovereign over all the cosmic realms. Christ as the agent of creation (Colossians 1:16) made this very structure; Paul in Ephesians 1:10 describes the goal of redemption as “bringing all things in heaven and on earth together under one head, even Christ” — the reconciliation of the cosmic separation into a unified new creation.
8: The second day establishes the heavens as God’s creation, not a rival divine realm. This demythologizing of the sky is the foundation for the NT affirmation that Christ rules over all heavenly powers: “he who descended is the very one who ascended higher than all the heavens, in order to fill the whole universe” (Ephesians 4:10). The heavens that God created are the realm Christ fills with his exalted presence after the ascension.
9: The gathering of waters and the appearing of dry land anticipates the new creation in which the sea gives up its dead and then disappears (Revelation 20:13; Genesis 21:1). Christ’s authority over water throughout the Gospels — calming storms (Mark 4:39), walking on the sea (Matthew 14:26), turning water to wine (John 2:9) — signals his identity as the Creator who made and governs the waters. The dry land that appears at God’s word is the platform for the human story that culminates in Christ.
10: God names the land and the seas — naming expresses authority and relationship. The theme of divine naming and renaming runs through Scripture: God renames Abram to Abraham, Jacob to Israel, and ultimately gives his people a new name through Christ (Revelation 2:17; Isaiah 62:2). This verse does not directly anticipate Christ but establishes the sovereign-naming pattern that the NT develops in Christ’s authority to name and claim his people.
11: The seed-bearing plants are the first instance of the seed motif that carries deep Christological freight. The “seed” (zeraʿ) language runs from here through the Abrahamic promise (“your seed”, Genesis 22:18) to Paul’s identification of Christ as “the seed” of Abraham (Galatians 3:16). Jesus uses seed imagery for the kingdom (Matthew 13:3–9; John 12:24 — “unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies”), and Paul uses it for resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:36–38). The seed principle established in creation is the biological template for the redemptive arc.
12: Plants bearing seed “according to their kinds” establishes the principle of organic continuity: life reproduces according to its nature. Paul applies this principle in 1 Corinthians 15:38–44 to the resurrection: the resurrection body grows from the mortal body as a plant grows from a seed, but in a different form. The creation principle of seed-to-plant continuity-and-transformation is the framework for understanding Christ’s resurrection and ours.
13: The third day established vegetation and is the day of the first “double good” in the creation account (God saw it was good at both v10 and v12 — the only day with a double affirmation). Rabbinic tradition noted that the third day is uniquely blessed. Without pressing the pattern allegorically, the third day has an eschatological resonance: Christ rises on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:4), the day of fullness and completion. The double-good of the third creation day does not predict the resurrection but the pattern of “third day” significance pervades Scripture.
14: The luminaries “serve as signs to mark sacred times (mōʿăḏîm), and days and years.” Paul in Colossians 2:16–17 states that the religious calendar — festivals, new moons, Sabbaths — is “a shadow of the things that were to come; the reality, however, is found in Christ.” The lights ordained to govern the sacred times are the basis for the entire Levitical festival system, which the NT reads as typologically fulfilled in Christ: Passover (1 Corinthians 5:7), Firstfruits (1 Corinthians 15:20), Tabernacles (John 7:37–39), Day of Atonement (Heb 9). All the appointed times find their reality in him.
15: The lights give light on the earth — their purpose is illumination, not adornment. Christ as the true light (John 1:9) fulfills what the created luminaries signify. The contrast between created light and the uncreated Light runs through John’s Gospel: the sun and moon are replaceable (Revelation 21:23 — the Lamb is the lamp of the new Jerusalem); Christ is the permanent, eternal illumination of God’s presence. This verse does not directly anticipate Christ but is part of the light-framework that the NT applies to him.
16: A shadow: the greater light governing the day points toward Christ as the one who governs with the full brightness of God’s glory. The lesser light governing the night may point toward the church that reflects Christ’s light as the moon reflects the sun (Matthew 5:14 — “you are the light of the world”; the church reflects what it does not generate). The new Jerusalem needs no sun or moon “for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp” (Revelation 21:23) — the created lights are replaced by the uncreated light of Christ himself.
17: God sets the lights in the expanse to give light on the earth. The placement language — God setting the lights in their positions — prefigures the way Paul describes Christ: “God placed all things under his feet and appointed him to be head over everything” (Ephesians 1:22). The Creator who placed the luminaries in authority is the same God who exalts Christ to universal authority; the structural principle of divine appointment to rule is the same in both cases.
18: The luminaries govern the day and night and separate light from darkness. Christ’s final governance will complete this separation permanently: in the new creation “there will be no night there” (Revelation 21:25; Genesis 22:5) and the light of Christ will govern without interruption. The rhythm of light and dark governing creation is a shadow of the eschatological reality where Christ’s light reigns without the darkness that currently alternates with it.
19: The fourth day is complete. The created order that Christ will redeem and transform is being established. This transitional verse does not require a specific Christological connection — it is part of the good creation through which God’s character and redemptive purpose will be expressed. The creation that God calls good is the world into which the Word will be incarnated (John 1:14), and which Christ will ultimately transform into the new creation (2 Corinthians 5:17; Revelation 21:5).
20: The teeming sea creatures and birds are part of the creation that Christ made (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16) and that groans for redemption (Romans 8:22). Directly, they do not anticipate Christ; as part of the good creation, they are the world that will be liberated from its bondage to decay when God’s children are revealed in glory through Christ (Romans 8:21). The fish and birds that obeyed Jesus’ fishing instructions (Luke 5:6; John 21:6) show his lordship over what he made.
21: The great sea creatures (tannînim — which can mean sea monsters or dragons in other OT contexts) are created by God and declared good — not hostile divine powers but created beings. This demythologizing anticipates the NT’s treatment of all cosmic powers as creatures subject to Christ: “whether thrones or powers or rulers or authorities; all things have been created through him and for him” (Colossians 1:16). What appears threatening in creation is within the scope of what Christ made and governs.
22: The first divine blessing in Scripture — “Be fruitful and increase in number and fill the water in the seas” — establishes fruitfulness as God’s intent for creation. Christ is the one through whom the Abrahamic fruitfulness promise is fulfilled globally: “in your seed all nations of the earth will be blessed” (Genesis 22:18; Galatians 3:8). The gospel produces fruit in all the world (Colossians 1:6). The fruitfulness mandate of creation is restored and exceeded in Christ’s mission.
23: The fifth day concludes. Within the creation narrative, this is a transitional marker. The creation is progressing toward the sixth day when the image-bearer — the one who will ultimately be restored in Christ — is created. The created creatures of sea and sky reflect God’s generative abundance; that abundance is a dim echo of the creative fullness that Christ both embodies and restores.
24: The land animals are the last category of creature before the creation of humanity. Christ, who created all things, “became flesh” (John 1:14) — entering the same biological creation whose origins are narrated here. The incarnation is the Creator entering his own creation; the land animals and humans are creatures of the same sixth day, but humanity uniquely bears the divine image. Christ comes as the true image-bearer to restore what sin distorted.
25: God sees that the animal creation is good. The goodness of the creation that Christ will redeem is established: sin does not destroy its fundamental goodness but mars and groans it. Paul in Romans 8:20–22 insists the creation was “subjected to futility, not by its own choice” — implying it was not originally futile. The good creation of v25 is the creation that Paul says waits “in eager expectation for the children of God to be revealed” through Christ’s redemption.
27: “God created mankind in his own image.” Paul identifies Christ as the perfect image of God (Colossians 1:15 — “the image of the invisible God”; 2 Corinthians 4:4 — “the image of God”). The first Adam was created in God’s image; Christ is that image without distortion, in its full divine expression. The restoration trajectory: sinners are being “transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory” (2 Corinthians 3:18) and will ultimately be conformed “to the image of his Son” (Romans 8:29). Male and female both bear the image — Galatians 3:28 (“neither male nor female” in Christ) is the redemption of the gendered image-bearing that began here.
28: The dominion mandate given to humanity is explicitly applied to Christ in the NT. Psalms 8:6 (“you made them rulers over the works of your hands”) echoes this verse; Hebrews 2:8–9 quotes Ps 8 and says “we do not yet see everything subject to them, but we do see Jesus, who was made lower than the angels for a little while, now crowned with glory and honor because he suffered death.” Humanity has not fulfilled the dominion mandate; Christ fulfills it. Matthew 28:18 (“all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me”) is the resurrection-declaration that Christ has received the dominion that Adam forfeited.
29: God’s provision of food for humanity — seed-bearing plants and fruit trees — is the original gift of sustenance. Christ as the “bread of life” (John 6:35, 48) is the ultimate divine provision: “I am the living bread that came down from heaven. Whoever eats this bread will live forever” (John 6:51). The garden’s generous provision of food is the type; Christ himself — not merely food — is the antitype. The eucharistic echo is also present: the bread Christ gives is his flesh for the life of the world.
30: God provides food for all living creatures — the scope of divine provision is universal and generous. The creation’s provision anticipates the new creation’s abundance: the tree of life yielding twelve crops of fruit (Revelation 22:2) and the river of the water of life flowing from God’s throne through Christ (Revelation 22:1). The original good creation where God provides for all creatures is the pattern for the new creation that Christ will bring — “all things new” (Revelation 21:5).
31: “God saw all that he had made, and it was very good.” The creation that God declares “very good” is the creation that Christ enters in the incarnation and will ultimately restore in the new creation. The movement from “very good” (Genesis 1:31) to “cursed” (Genesis 3:17–18) to “all things new” (Revelation 21:5) is the arc of redemptive history. Christ’s “it is finished” (John 19:30) echoes the completion language of creation week; his resurrection is the beginning of the new creation that will ultimately be declared very good once more.
